The law, which was a carve-out of the European Union’s ePrivacy Directive, was put in place in 2021 as a temporary measure allowing companies to use automated detection technologies to scan messages for harms, including child sexual abuse material (CSAM), grooming and sextortion . However, it expired on 3 April, and the EU parliament decided not to vote to extend it, amid privacy concerns from some lawmakers. This article was amended on 10 April 2026 to correct the name of the EU’s ePrivacy Directive. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Elon Musk’s xAI sues Colorado over new rules for artificial intelligence
source_signals.summary: Company claims law regulating AI systems, set to go into effect in June, infringes on its first amendment rights Elon Musk ’s artificial intelligence company, xAI, has filed a lawsuit against the state of Colorado over a new AI law set to take effect in June. The suit seeks to block the state from enforcing the law, which would impose new requirements on AI systems to protect state residents from “algorithmic discrimination” in sectors such as education, employment, healthcare, housing and financial services. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: ‘Disbelief and disappointment’: how Javier Milei’s bribery scandal may have derailed Argentina’s crypto investment
source_signals.summary: Just as the industry is set to capitalize on country’s political and economic instability, president accused in $5m scheme The Argentinian president, Javier Milei , is facing his lowest approval ratings since taking office in 2023 as newly published evidence allegedly reveals a $5m financial agreement connected to his public endorsement last year of a controversial crypto project. The scandal has tarnished crypto’s reputation across Argentina and set back the ambitions of industry insiders who saw the country as fertile soil for the growth of digital money. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Readers reply: Should we be polite to voice assistants and AIs?
source_signals.summary: The long-running series in which readers answer other readers’ questions on subjects ranging from trivial flights of fancy to profound scientific and philosophical concepts This week’s question: what would the world look like if people didn’t make mistakes? I always say please and thank you to my Alexa. Why is this? I am sure it doesn’t care. Is it worth being polite to artificial assistants? Alison Williams, Toronto Send new questions to nq@theguardian.com . Continue reading...

source_signals.title: AI companies know they have an image problem. Will funding policy papers and thinktanks dig them out?
source_signals.summary: The aggressive effort by major players aims to reshape the narrative as polls show increasing public disapproval of AI OpenAI made a surprise announcement this week – not an update to ChatGPT or another multibillion-dollar datacenter – but a policy paper that called for a reimagining of the social contract based around “a slate of people-first ideas”. It’s the latest move in an aggressive effort by the major AI players to reshape the narrative around their industry, as polls show public disapproval of AI increasing. OpenAI’s 13-page paper, titled Industrial Policy for the Intelligence Age , follows its surprise acquisition of tech-friendly podcast TBPN and its announcement of plans to open a Washington DC office that will feature a dedicated space called the OpenAI workshop for non-profits and policymakers to learn about and discuss the company’s technology. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: ‘Too powerful for the public’: Inside Anthropic’s bid to win the AI publicity war
source_signals.summary: The firm says it withheld an AI model on cybersecurity grounds but sceptics say this was hype to lure investment This week, the AI company Anthropic said it had created an AI model so powerful that, out of a sense of overwhelming responsibility, it was not going to release it to the public. The US treasury secretary, Scott Bessent, summoned the heads of major banks for a chat about the model, Mythos. The Reform UK MP Danny Kruger wrote a letter to the government urging it to “ engage with AI firm Anthropic whose new frontier model Claude Mythos could present catastrophic cybersecurity risks to the UK”. X went wild. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: iPhone 17e review: Apple upgrades its cheapest new smartphone
source_signals.summary: Mid-range handset gets chip, storage and MagSafe upgrades to offer more essential iOS features for less The cheapest new iPhone has been upgraded for this year with a faster chip, double the storage, automatic portraits and MagSafe, providing even more of the core Apple smartphone experience for less. The iPhone 17e is an upgraded version of the mid-range “e” line launched last year with the first iPhone 16e and is the latest member of the iPhone 17 family. It starts at £599 (€699/$599/A$999), undercutting the iPhone 17 and iPhone 16 by £200 and £100 respectively to be the cheapest new iPhone sold by Apple. Screen: 6.1in Super Retina XDR (OLED) (460ppi) Processor: Apple A19 (4-core GPU) RAM: 8GB Storage: 256 or 512GB Operating system: iOS 26 Camera: 48MP rear; 12MP front-facing Connectivity: 5G, wifi 6, NFC, Bluetooth 5.3, USB-C, Satellite and GNSS Water resistance: IP68 (6 metres for 30 mins) Dimensions: 146.7 x 71.5 x 7.8mm Weight: 170g Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra review: its huge screen blocks shoulder surfers from spying on you
source_signals.summary: Latest Android superphone packs great cameras, fast chips, long battery, a stylus and first-of-its-kind privacy display Samsung’s latest Ultra superphone promises to keep shoulder surfers out of your business with a first-of-its-kind privacy display built into its huge 6.9in screen. The Galaxy S26 Ultra is Samsung’s top-of-the-line phone costing £1,279 (€1,449/$1,299/A$2,199) and is one of the most feature-packed handsets you can get, with four cameras on the back, an integrated stylus and AI assistance in every corner. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Google Pixel 10a review: cheaper Android is great, but no real advance
source_signals.summary: Quality camera, good software and long battery life, but you should just buy the Pixel 9a instead The latest smartphone in the lower-cost A-series Pixel line shows what makes Google phones so good, while undercutting the competition on price. The problem is that it differs little from its predecessor, which is still on sale. Priced from £499 (€549/$499/A$849), the Pixel 10a is more like a second edition of last year’s excellent Pixel 9a . The two phones share the same Tensor G4 chip, not the newer G5 in the rest of the £799 and up Pixel 10 line; the same memory, storage and cameras; the same size 6.3in OLED screen, though the Pixel 10a reaches a higher peak brightness making it slightly easier to read outside. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Fairphone 6 review: cheaper, repairable and longer-lasting Android
source_signals.summary: Sustainable smartphone takes a step forward with modular accessories, a good screen and mid-range performance The Dutch ethical smartphone brand Fairphone is back with its six-generation Android, aiming to make its repairable phone more modern, modular, affordable and desirable, with screw-in accessories and a user-replaceable battery. The Fairphone 6 costs £499 (€599), making it cheaper than previous models and pitting it squarely against budget champs such as the Google Pixel 9a and the Nothing Phone 3a Pro , while being repairable at home with long-term software support and a five-year warranty. On paper it sounds like the ideal phone to see out the decade. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: MacBook Neo review: the budget Apple laptop powered by an iPhone chip
source_signals.summary: Snappy performance, high-quality screen, best-in-class keyboard and trackpad show cheaper can still be great Apple’s brand new entry-level laptop is powered by the chip from an iPhone and offers more than just the essential MacBook experience for a great price, putting the PC industry on notice. The MacBook Neo is the first of its kind from Apple. A 13in laptop that runs on an A18 Pro chip and brings the starting price for a brand new MacBook down to £599 (€699/$599/A$899) – £500 or the equivalent less than the MacBook Air. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Apple iPad Air M4 review: still the premium tablet to beat
source_signals.summary: Faster laptop-level power, rapid wifi and 5G, plus much-improved multitasking make the middle iPad highly capable beyond just watching TV The latest iPad Air is faster in almost all facets, packing not just a processor upgrade but improvements to most of the internal bits that make the tablet work, providing laptop-grade power in a skinny, adaptable touchscreen device. The new iPad Air M4 costs from the same £599 (€649/$599/A$999) as the outgoing M3 model from last year and again comes in two sizes. One with an 11in screen, which is the best size for most people and a more expensive 13in screen version, which is ideal if you want a second TV or a laptop replacement. source_signals.title: Logitech MX Master 4 review: the best work mouse you can buy
source_signals.summary: Ergonomic shape, quality materials and satisfying clicks, now with novel haptic feedback and repairable design Logitech’s latest productivity power-house updates one of the greatest mice of all time with smoother materials, a repair-friendly design and a haptic motor for phone-like vibrations on your desktop. The MX Master 4 is the latest evolution in a line of pioneering mice that dates back more than 20 years and has long been the mouse to beat for everything but hardcore PC gaming. Having given it a magnetic free-spinning scroll wheel, plenty of buttons and precise tracking, now Logitech is trying something different for its seven-generation: the ability to tap back at you. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Super Mario what?! The seven best obscure Mario games
source_signals.summary: As The Super Mario Galaxy Movie storms the box office, we look back at the best forgotten games inspired by Tetris, Lemmings and … vitamins? It should be no surprise that the latest Super Mario movie is smashing box office records – despite the, let’s say mixed, reviews . Nintendo’s iconic plumber has been a pop culture staple for 45 years, starring in some of the bestselling video games ever made, from the original Donkey Kong through to the joyous Super Mario Bros Wonder and the chaotic Mario Kart World. But as with any storied showbiz career, there have been some lesser works. Who can forget – or actually remember – Hotel Mario, a door-shutting puzzle game for the doomed Philips CD-i console? Or what about Mario Teaches Typing, a 1992 educational game for the PC in which players navigate the Mushroom Kingdom by … correctly inputting words. Yet there have also been genuine treasures lost along the way. Here, then, are seven of our favourite much-overlooked Mario odysseys. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: The Masters 2026: McIlroy, Scheffler, Rose and Young in contention in thrilling final round – live
source_signals.summary: ️ Updates from the final round at Augusta National ️ Ewan Murray’s day-three report | Leaderboard | Mail Scott ️ García warned after breaking club in meltdown Marco Penge was making a good fist of his Masters debut. Especially as the 27-year-old from Crawley, the reigning Spanish Open champion, took a triple-bogey eight at the 2nd on Thursday. Not the most auspicious start to his Augusta National career, but he limited the first-round damage to 76, then shot 69 and 71. Sadly his final round isn’t going so well, and he’s just dumped two balls in the water at the iconic par-three 12th, the first spinning back off the bank, the second from the dropzone not even getting over to dry land before dunking into the drink. A quadruple-bogey seven. He isn’t the first, he won’t be the last, and things could have gotten a whole lot worse, just ask the Towering Inferno … Bogey at the last for Jon Rahm. A diminuendo end to a fine round of 68. You have to wonder how much buyer’s remorse Rahmbo has for joining the LIV tour: the 2021 US Open champion and 2023 Masters winner has never been the same player since. Still, his recovery this week from an opening round of 78 will give him a little succour. He ends his week at +1, one shy of the current clubhouse leader Gary Woodland. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Manchester City close gap in title race with emphatic victory at Chelsea
source_signals.summary: After a season dominated by long throws, tortured set-piece routines and rigid football, a killer blend of artistry and firepower is threatening to deliver another domestic treble for Manchester City. They have stirred when it matters most, crushing Liverpool in the FA Cup last weekend, and have their gaze firmly fixed on wounded prey after closing the gap on Arsenal with an expert destruction of Chelsea at a flat Stamford Bridge. Pep Guardiola’s sharks could smell the blood in the water after Arsenal’s stumble against Bournemouth . City were ferocious and unstoppable. They took this game away from a callow and careless Chelsea with a blistering surge at the start of the second half and, even if there are twists and turns to come, there is no doubt this emphatic 3-0 win has tilted momentum in the title race to Guardiola’s side. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Wout van Aert outduels Tadej Pogacar to win his first Paris-Roubaix title
source_signals.summary: Belgian ends decade-old run of bad luck in event Franziska Koch beats Marianne Vos in women’s race Wout van Aert shattered a decade-old jinx to win Paris-Roubaix on Sunday, outduelling the world champion, Tadej Pogacar, in a brutal classic race across the cobbles, while Franziska Koch edged out Marianne Vos in the women’s race. The 31-year-old sustained a puncture as did Pogacar and their great rival Mathieu van der Poel, who had two mechanical problems and could not contest the final sprint, which went in Van Aert’s favour for his second title in one of the five Monument classics after his Milan-Sanremo victory in 2020. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Jannik Sinner beats Carlos Alcaraz at Monte Carlo Masters to reclaim world No 1 spot
source_signals.summary: Italian wins 7-6 (5), 6-3 against Spanish rival Sinner adds to his victories after Indian Wells and Miami Jannik Sinner beat Carlos Alcaraz in straight sets to win the Monte Carlo Masters for the first time on Sunday and reclaim the world No 1 ranking from his Spanish rival. Sinner downed Alcaraz 7-6 (5), 6-3 to capture his third ATP 1000 title of the year after completing the “Sunshine Double” last month with victories in Indian Wells and Miami. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Fury only wants Joshua but after 10 years of wrangling will superfight ever happen?
source_signals.summary: ‘It’s either him or I’m out,’ Fury said of his British heavyweight rival after outclassing Arslanbek Makhmudov Just before midnight on Saturday, an hour after the first victory of his latest comeback , Tyson Fury paused in the midst of a familiar monologue about a predictable subject. He had just outclassed Arslanbek Makhmudov, the tough but limited Russian heavyweight, over 12 one‑sided rounds. But Fury wanted to talk only about Anthony Joshua and whether or not he and his old British rival would ever fight each other. In the lavish depths of Tottenham Hotspur Stadium, Fury sounded perplexed: “Ten years in the making and still, after all this time, there’s uncertainty if this fight’s gonna happen next. I’ve no idea. I hope so but you can’t force someone to do something.” Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Welcome to Pep in April – the serial title avenger with Arsenal in his sights | Barney Ronay
source_signals.summary: Manchester City’s unbeaten April record in the past four years bodes well for their end-of-season pursuit for glory “I have a particular set of skills. Skills I have acquired over a very long career. I may stumble a little in the autumn. I may get a little caustic with a TV camera crew or sarcastically applaud a referee. But I will pursue you. lead_story.title: Trump's blockade threat raises risks and leaves predicaments unchanged
lead_story.summary: The Strait of Hormuz threat escalates the stakes without resolving the underlying conflict, leaving Iran, the US and allies to weigh deterrence, escalation and the cost of disruption to global energy flows.
lead_story.source_signal_title: Trump's blockade threat raises risks and leaves predicaments unchanged
body: Donald Trump’s threat to blockade the Strait of Hormuz has intensified an already volatile stand-off between Washington and Tehran, but it has not resolved the deeper problem that brought the two sides to this point. The strait is one of the world’s most important energy chokepoints, and even the prospect of disruption is enough to unsettle governments, insurers, shippers and markets far beyond the Gulf.

In practical terms, any attempt to block or impede traffic through the waterway would carry immediate risks of military escalation. Iran has long treated Hormuz as a pressure point, while the US and its allies have made freedom of navigation a red line. That leaves both sides with incentives to signal resolve without stumbling into a broader conflict they may struggle to control.

For Iran, the threat may be meant to show it can still impose costs despite air strikes and sanctions. For Mr Trump, it projects toughness and seeks to raise the price of defiance. But threats of this kind can also narrow the space for diplomacy, especially if either side feels compelled to answer force with force to avoid looking weak.

The immediate economic impact is already visible in higher energy prices and greater anxiety in shipping and aviation. Even short-lived disruption in Hormuz can ripple quickly through oil markets, transport costs and inflation expectations. That is why governments from Europe to Asia are watching the waterway so closely, even if they are not direct parties to the confrontation.

Yet the underlying strategic predicaments remain unchanged. The US still wants to deter Iran without being drawn into a longer war, while Iran wants sanctions relief and security guarantees without conceding too much under pressure. Allies are caught between supporting Washington, protecting trade routes and avoiding steps that could deepen the crisis.

That makes the blockade threat less a solution than a further escalation of the test of wills already under way. The danger now is not only whether Hormuz is physically closed, but whether enough actors start behaving as if it might be, with all the economic and military consequences that would follow.

section_features.title: After Iran talks falter, the big question is what happens next?
section_features.standfirst: The ceasefire has not removed the central strategic problem: how to stop the conflict widening while keeping energy routes open and diplomacy alive.

section_features.label: Markets feature
section_features.title: Collapse of US-Iran talks heightens fears of prolonged energy shock
section_features.standfirst: Oil, shipping, borrowing costs and consumer prices are all exposed if the Gulf remains disrupted and the ceasefire weakens further.

section_features.label: Technology feature
section_features.title: AI companies know they have an image problem. Will funding policy papers and thinktanks dig them out?
section_features.standfirst: The sector is trying to shape regulation and public opinion at once, but trust, safety and commercial power remain unresolved.

stories.title: Civilians feared killed after reports of air strike on Nigerian market
stories.summary: Reports of a Nigerian Air Force strike on a village market have triggered concerns over civilian casualties and the conduct of operations against militants in the north-east.
stories.source_signal_title: Civilians feared killed after reports of air strike on Nigerian market
body: Reports that the Nigerian Air Force struck a village market in the country’s north-east have raised fears that civilians were killed while operations were under way against militants in the region. The military has not yet publicly addressed the allegation, and details of what happened remain unclear.

The incident is significant because Nigeria has spent years fighting insurgent groups in the north-east, where military operations have often taken place in areas where armed fighters and civilians live alongside one another. That creates constant risk of mistakes, especially when strikes are carried out on or near busy public places.

If the reports are confirmed, the episode is likely to renew scrutiny of how Nigerian forces identify targets and warn communities before attacks. Rights groups and local residents have repeatedly accused security forces of poor accountability in the conduct of the conflict, particularly when civilian harm is alleged.

The immediate questions are how many people were killed, whether the target was correctly identified and whether the strike was a misfire. Those answers may take time to emerge, but the political and humanitarian consequences are already apparent: any civilian deaths would deepen distrust in the military and complicate the wider campaign against militants.

stories.title: Ukraine and Russia accuse each other of hundreds of ceasefire violations
stories.summary: Both sides say the other has broken the truce repeatedly, underlining how fragile any pause in the fighting remains despite calls for restraint.
stories.source_signal_title: Ukraine and Russia accuse each other of hundreds of ceasefire violations
body: Ukraine and Russia have each accused the other of breaching a ceasefire hundreds of times, underlining how fragile any pause in the fighting remains. The accusations come against a backdrop of repeated breakdowns in limited truces since Russia’s full-scale invasion of Ukraine began.

President Volodymyr Zelensky said Ukrainian forces would respond symmetrically if attacked, a warning that reflects the low level of confidence on both sides. Even when a truce is announced, neither side appears prepared to trust the other enough to step back for long.

That matters because ceasefires can only work if both parties believe there is a credible cost to breaking them and a realistic path to a wider settlement. At present, neither condition appears in place, so even a temporary pause risks becoming another brief interruption rather than a foundation for talks.

stories.title: At least 30 feared dead in crush at Haitian tourist site
stories.summary: A deadly crush at a crowded tourist event has renewed concern over public safety, emergency response and political instability in Haiti.
stories.source_signal_title: At least 30 feared dead in crush at Haitian tourist site
body: At least 30 people are feared dead after a crush at a crowded tourist event in Haiti, according to the country’s prime minister. The incident involved many young people and has also left others injured or missing, deepening a tragedy at a time when public services are already under severe strain.

The scale of the crush is a grim reminder of how vulnerable large public gatherings can be in Haiti, where political instability, weak infrastructure and limited emergency capacity make any mass-casualty event harder to manage. When an accident happens in such conditions, the consequences can be especially severe.

What remains uncertain is the full death toll and whether the incident was caused by overcrowding, a security failure or another trigger. But the wider significance is already clear: Haiti’s chronic instability is not only political but practical, shaping how safely people can gather, travel and respond when disaster strikes.

stories.title: After Iran talks falter, the big question is what happens next?
stories.summary: The collapse of direct talks leaves the US and Iran facing the same hard choice as before: escalation, containment or a return to negotiations under far more dangerous conditions.
stories.source_signal_title: After Iran talks falter, the big question is what happens next?
body: The failure of direct talks between the US and Iran leaves both sides facing the same dilemma they were trying to avoid: escalation, containment or a return to negotiations under worse conditions. Twenty-one hours of diplomacy were not enough to bridge nearly five decades of hostility, and the breakdown now carries consequences well beyond the negotiating room.

For Washington, the challenge is to preserve pressure on Iran without triggering a wider conflict that would raise energy prices and unsettle allies. For Tehran, the choice is whether to absorb more blows, escalate in return, or seek a new diplomatic opening from a position of weakness. Each option carries costs, and none offers a clean exit.

That is why the next phase matters as much as the talks themselves. If neither side wants open-ended confrontation, some form of indirect contact may resume, but it is likely to do so under much more dangerous conditions than before.

stories.title: Germany's far-right AfD adopts 'radical' manifesto ahead of key polls
stories.summary: The AfD is sharpening its message before key state elections, a sign of how far-right politics continues to gain traction in parts of Germany.
stories.source_signal_title: Germany's far-right AfD adopts 'radical' manifesto ahead of key polls
body: Germany’s Alternative for Germany, or AfD, has adopted a more radical manifesto as it prepares for key state elections, sharpening a strategy that has helped it gain ground in parts of the country. The party is particularly strong in Saxony-Anhalt, where it is riding high in opinion polls.

The move matters because it shows the AfD continuing to push a hard-line message on migration, identity and national politics despite years of controversy and scrutiny from opponents and much of the mainstream press. Far-right politics has remained resilient in sections of eastern Germany, where resentment over economic and political change has been fertile ground for the party.

The question now is whether the tougher line widens its appeal further or hardens resistance to it. Either way, the manifesto underlines that the AfD is not moderating in the run-up to the polls; it is intensifying the same politics that have made it one of Germany’s most consequential and divisive parties.

stories.title: Asha Bhosle: The sound of Bollywood dies aged 92
stories.summary: The death of Asha Bhosle marks the loss of one of Indian cinema’s defining voices, whose work shaped popular culture across generations.
stories.source_signal_title: Asha Bhosle: The sound of Bollywood dies aged 92
body: Asha Bhosle, one of Indian cinema’s most distinctive and enduring voices, has died aged 92. For generations of listeners, her songs were inseparable from the sound of Bollywood itself, carrying across films, radios, weddings and popular culture in India and far beyond.

Her significance was not just in the size of her catalogue, but in the emotional range and recognisability of her singing. She helped define an era in which playback singers were central to the imagination of Hindi cinema, and her work became part of the shared cultural memory of millions.

Her death marks the loss of an artist whose influence stretched over decades and whose voice connected different generations. For many fans, it will feel less like the end of a career than the closing of a chapter in modern Indian cultural history.

stories.title: Collapse of US-Iran talks heightens fears of prolonged energy shock
stories.summary: The failure of diplomacy has intensified fears of higher oil prices, costlier borrowing and renewed inflation pressure across the world economy.
stories.source_signal_title: Collapse of US-Iran talks heightens fears of prolonged energy shock
body: The collapse of direct talks between the US and Iran has deepened fears that the shock to global energy markets will last longer than many hoped. With diplomacy stalled, traders are left to price in the risk of higher oil costs, possible disruption to shipping and a broader hit to confidence.

That matters because energy is feeding quickly into other parts of the economy. Higher fuel prices can lift transport bills, raise production costs and complicate central banks’ efforts to bring inflation down. The effects are rarely confined to one country; they tend to move through supply chains and consumer prices across regions.

Europe, Asia and the US are each exposed in different ways, but all face the same basic problem: uncertainty. Airlines worry about jet fuel, manufacturers about freight and households about what they pay at the pump. If the crisis persists, those costs may become harder to reverse even if fighting does not spread further.

For policymakers, the test is whether to treat the disruption as temporary or prepare for a more prolonged squeeze. A ceasefire would ease some pressure, but unless it is followed by a workable diplomatic reset, markets are likely to remain volatile and the inflationary impact could linger.

stories.title: Soaring pump prices drive US inflation to highest level in almost two years
stories.summary: Higher fuel costs linked to the conflict have pushed US inflation upwards, complicating the outlook for households and policymakers alike.
stories.source_signal_title: Soaring pump prices drive US inflation to highest level in almost two years
body: US inflation has risen to its highest level in almost two years as higher petrol prices linked to the conflict with Iran fed through to consumers. The increase adds to pressure on households already facing persistent living-cost strains.

The latest figures matter because fuel prices tend to move quickly into broader inflation readings, affecting transport, shipping and the cost of everyday goods. That in turn complicates the outlook for the Federal Reserve, which must weigh weaker growth risks against the possibility of renewed price pressure.

For Americans, the immediate impact is being felt at the pump. For policymakers, the harder problem is whether the rise proves brief or becomes part of a wider inflationary rebound if energy markets remain unsettled.

stories.title: EU airline industry warns of fuel shortages if Strait of Hormuz stays closed
stories.summary: European aviation groups warn that prolonged disruption in the Gulf could tighten jet fuel supply and raise operating costs.
stories.source_signal_title: EU airline industry warns of fuel shortages if Strait of Hormuz stays closed
body: European aviation groups have warned that prolonged disruption in the Strait of Hormuz could tighten jet fuel supplies and increase operating costs. The alert reflects the industry’s dependence on steady fuel flows through one of the most important routes for Gulf energy exports.

Airlines are exposed in two ways: first through the cost of fuel itself, and then through the knock-on effects on ticket prices, route planning and margins. Even if supplies are not cut off completely, a sustained squeeze could force carriers to absorb higher bills or pass them on to passengers.

The warning is another sign that the crisis is now being priced well beyond the Gulf. Aviation, like shipping and manufacturing, depends on predictable access to fuel, and the longer the uncertainty lasts, the more likely it is that business decisions will be shaped by the risk of further disruption.

stories.title: Tankers urged not to pay toll to Iran for use of strait
stories.summary: Shipping interests are being advised against paying levies for safe passage, highlighting the legal and commercial uncertainty around the waterway.
stories.source_signal_title: Tankers urged not to pay toll to Iran for use of strait
body: Tanker firms are being advised not to pay any Iranian levy in return for safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz, highlighting the legal uncertainty surrounding movement through the waterway. The advice reflects concern that paying could legitimise a demand that shipping interests may later regret.

The issue matters because it sits at the intersection of commercial necessity and geopolitical pressure. Shipping companies want to keep vessels moving, but they also need to avoid setting precedents that could expose them to future claims or sanctions-related complications.

What happens next will depend on whether insurers, shipowners and flag states conclude that the route remains workable without concessions. If not, the strait could become even less usable, not simply because of military risk but because of the uncertainty around the rules governing passage.

stories.title: How many ships are crossing the Strait of Hormuz?
stories.summary: BBC Verify analysis suggests traffic through the strait remains severely limited, a sign of how fragile the route has become.
stories.source_signal_title: How many ships are crossing the Strait of Hormuz?
body: BBC Verify analysis suggests that traffic through the Strait of Hormuz remains severely limited, with only a small number of vessels crossing since the ceasefire deal. That is significant because the strait is normally a vital corridor for global oil and gas shipments.

The low level of traffic shows how quickly a political crisis can affect commercial behaviour even without a formal closure. Shipowners, insurers and crews tend to act cautiously when they believe a route may become dangerous, which can reduce traffic before any official blockade is in place.

The open question is whether this is a temporary slowdown or the start of a longer rerouting of trade. If vessels stay away, the economic consequences could deepen regardless of whether the strait is legally open, because markets respond to perceived risk as well as physical obstruction.

stories.title: UK farmers warn Iran ceasefire too late to stop higher food costs
stories.summary: Farmers say the conflict has already fed through to energy, freight and fertiliser costs, limiting any quick relief for food prices.
stories.source_signal_title: UK farmers warn Iran ceasefire too late to stop higher food costs
body: UK farmers say the Iran conflict has already pushed up the costs of energy, freight and fertiliser, meaning any ceasefire is unlikely to bring immediate relief to food prices. The warning reflects the way global shocks work through agriculture long after the headlines move on.

Farming is energy-intensive and heavily exposed to transport and input prices. When oil and gas become more expensive, so do haulage, packaging and fertiliser, and those costs can take time to unwind even if the broader crisis eases.

That leaves consumers with another reminder that food prices often respond slowly to geopolitical events. A ceasefire may calm markets, but it cannot quickly reverse the bills that have already been passed along the supply chain.

stories.title: Faisal Islam: Why the government is relaxed about Chinese car imports
stories.summary: The government sees Chinese cars as both a consumer benefit and a possible industrial challenge, reflecting the wider tension between openness and protection in trade policy.
stories.source_signal_title: Faisal Islam: Why the government is relaxed about Chinese car imports
body: The UK government’s relatively relaxed stance on Chinese car imports reflects a calculation that cheaper vehicles can benefit consumers even if they create pressure for domestic manufacturers. It is a pragmatic position, but one that sits uneasily with broader concerns about industrial resilience and strategic dependence.

Chinese electric cars are part of a much wider trade dilemma. Governments want to support competition, keep prices down and accelerate the transition to cleaner transport, yet they also worry about the fate of local supply chains and the risk of becoming overly reliant on one dominant producer.

The issue matters because it is not just about cars. It is about where Britain draws the line between openness and protection in an era of subsidy races, geopolitical tension and faster industrial change. The government appears to be betting that consumers will benefit first, and that industry can adapt later.

Whether that judgement holds will depend on how quickly domestic firms can compete on price, technology and scale. If they cannot, the policy may look less like openness and more like surrender to a structural advantage that is difficult to reverse.

stories.title: British Steel 'needs nationalising by the summer'
stories.summary: Pressure is growing over the future of British Steel as ownership talks continue and questions mount over the pace of intervention.
stories.source_signal_title: British Steel 'needs nationalising by the summer'
body: Pressure is mounting over the future of British Steel, with a Labour MP saying it needs to be nationalised by the summer as ownership talks continue. The intervention adds to a long-running debate over how best to secure the company’s future amid uncertainty about investment and control.

British Steel has become a test case for whether the state should step in when strategic industry is at risk. Steel remains central to construction, defence and infrastructure, so any prolonged instability at the company has implications beyond a single plant or balance sheet.

What happens next will depend on negotiations with owners, the scale of any rescue package and ministers’ appetite for direct intervention. But the call for nationalisation shows how little confidence there is that the current process can deliver a stable outcome quickly enough.

stories.title: OpenAI pauses UK data centre deal over energy costs and regulation
stories.summary: A planned investment has been delayed amid concerns over power prices and rules, a reminder that AI expansion depends on infrastructure and policy as much as demand.
stories.source_signal_title: OpenAI pauses UK data centre deal over energy costs and regulation
body: OpenAI has paused plans for a UK data centre deal, citing concerns over energy costs and regulation. The delay is a reminder that artificial intelligence expansion depends not only on demand for new services, but on power, planning and policy.

Large-scale AI infrastructure is energy-hungry, which makes electricity prices and access to grid capacity central to investment decisions. If those conditions look uncertain, companies may postpone projects even in markets they regard as strategically important.

The pause matters because the UK has been trying to position itself as an attractive place for AI investment. A delay does not amount to a withdrawal, but it does show that promises of a tech boom can run into very practical limits when costs and regulation do not align.

stories.title: This coat cost $248 in illegal tariffs. Will he ever get the money back?
stories.summary: The tariff-refund issue shows how trade disputes can leave importers and consumers waiting for clarity long after the legal questions are settled.
stories.source_signal_title: This coat cost $248 in illegal tariffs. Will he ever get the money back?
body: The dispute over tariff refunds shows how trade policy can leave importers and consumers waiting for clarity long after the legal questions have been settled. In this case, a coat that included $248 in illegal tariffs has become a symbol of the uncertainty that follows customs disputes.

The broader issue is that tariffs are rarely borne cleanly by one side. Importers may pay first, retailers may pass costs on, and consumers can be left asking who is actually entitled to a refund when a tariff is ruled unlawful. That can make restitution slow and complicated.

The case matters because it exposes a common weakness in trade enforcement: legal victories do not always translate into quick cash back in people’s hands. Even when the principle is clear, the practical route to repayment can be murky and contested.

stories.title: Starmer urges US and Iran to 'find way through' after peace talks falter
stories.summary: The prime minister is trying to project restraint as the Iran crisis deepens and pressure grows over Britain’s diplomatic and security posture.
stories.source_signal_title: Starmer urges US and Iran to 'find way through' after peace talks falter
body: Sir Keir Starmer has urged the US and Iran to find a way through their stalled negotiations, seeking to project calm as tensions around the conflict deepen. His intervention reflects Britain’s interest in avoiding further escalation while keeping diplomatic channels open.

The prime minister’s position is politically delicate. Britain is aligned with Washington on security, but it also has an interest in de-escalation because of the impact on energy markets, shipping and regional stability. That leaves London trying to sound supportive without appearing to endorse a wider war.

What happens next will depend on whether the two sides can resume contact and whether allies can help create space for talks. For now, the government is signalling restraint, but the scope for diplomacy appears narrower than before the negotiations failed.

stories.title: UK forced to shelve Chagos Islands legislation after US dropped support
stories.summary: The collapse of the legislation reflects the limits of British leverage when Washington withholds backing for a deal central to decolonisation talks.
stories.source_signal_title: UK forced to shelve Chagos Islands legislation after US dropped support
body: The UK has shelved legislation on the Chagos Islands after support from the US fell away, exposing the limits of British leverage in a dispute that sits at the intersection of decolonisation, diplomacy and strategic interests. The move is a setback for ministers who had hoped to advance a long-running settlement.

The issue matters because the islands are not just a constitutional question but a military and geopolitical one, given the importance of the Diego Garcia base. Without Washington’s backing, London’s room for manoeuvre narrowed sharply, and the legislation became politically and procedurally difficult to carry through.

For Mauritius, the pause is likely to be seen as another sign that the decolonisation process remains unfinished. For the UK, it is a reminder that even where there is a principle agreement in sight, external support can determine whether a deal survives the final stretch.

stories.title: Home Office to announce closure of 11 asylum hotels in next week
stories.summary: Ministers are moving to reduce hotel use for asylum housing, a politically sensitive policy shift that will test delivery and capacity.
stories.source_signal_title: Home Office to announce closure of 11 asylum hotels in next week
body: The Home Office is preparing to announce the closure of 11 asylum hotels, signalling a further attempt to reduce the use of temporary accommodation for people seeking refuge in the UK. The policy is politically sensitive because hotel use has become a flashpoint in debates over immigration, cost and local pressure.

The closures matter because asylum housing remains one of the most visible and contentious parts of the system. Ministers want to bring down costs and reduce reliance on hotels, but doing so requires alternative accommodation, administrative capacity and a faster pace of decision-making.

What remains uncertain is whether the closures mark the start of a sustained reduction or a limited rearrangement. If the Home Office cannot replace hotel places quickly enough, pressure is likely to shift elsewhere rather than disappear.

stories.title: Votes for populist parties in May elections will put NHS at risk, Streeting says
stories.summary: Streeting is framing the May contests around the health service, warning that protest voting could carry consequences for NHS policy and funding.
stories.source_signal_title: Votes for populist parties in May elections will put NHS at risk, Streeting says
body: Wes Streeting has warned that votes for populist parties in May’s elections could put the NHS at risk, framing the contests around the future of the health service. It is a clear attempt to make the elections about public services rather than protest politics.

The significance of the warning lies in the way Labour is trying to defend its position from parties that campaign on anger at the establishment. By linking those votes to NHS funding and policy, Streeting is arguing that ballot-box frustration could have direct consequences for a system already under pressure.

Whether that message lands will depend on how voters view the state of the health service and the broader political mood. But the intervention shows how central the NHS remains to British electoral politics, even in contests that might otherwise be read as local or mid-term tests.

stories.title: AI companies know they have an image problem. Will funding policy papers and thinktanks dig them out?
stories.summary: The AI industry is investing heavily in public-policy influence as scrutiny grows over safety, labour impacts and market power.
stories.source_signal_title: AI companies know they have an image problem. Will funding policy papers and thinktanks dig them out?
body: AI companies are investing heavily in thinktanks, policy papers and public debate as scrutiny grows over safety, labour displacement and market power. The industry clearly understands that technical progress alone will not settle public concern about what frontier AI is becoming.

That matters because the argument over AI is no longer just about product performance. It is about who gets to shape the rules, who funds the expertise behind them and whether public policy is being informed by independent analysis or by companies seeking legitimacy.

The use of policy influence is not unusual in itself, but the scale and speed of AI investment have made it more visible. As the technology becomes more powerful, the fight over reputation, regulation and narrative is likely to become as important as the race for capability.

What remains uncertain is whether this strategy can rebuild trust. Funding papers may improve access and credibility in policy circles, but it will not by itself answer the harder questions about control, accountability and the social effects of increasingly capable systems.

stories.title: ‘Too powerful for the public’: Inside Anthropic’s bid to win the AI publicity war
stories.summary: Anthropic’s claims about its latest model have prompted scepticism as well as alarm, illustrating the race to control the narrative around frontier AI.
stories.source_signal_title: ‘Too powerful for the public’: Inside Anthropic’s bid to win the AI publicity war
body: Anthropic’s latest claims about its model have drawn both attention and scepticism, highlighting the intensifying struggle among frontier AI firms to control the public narrative. As the technology advances, questions about safety and governance have become almost as important as the models themselves.

The company’s message appears designed to present its systems as powerful but responsible, at a moment when rivals are also trying to reassure regulators, customers and the public. That public-relations battle matters because trust is becoming a commercial asset in its own right.

What is uncertain is how much such claims can persuade outside the industry. In a field marked by secrecy, hype and rapid change, scepticism is likely to remain high, especially when companies are asking the public to accept that systems are both transformative and safely contained.

stories.title: ‘Your photos will be deleted’: Apple users warned over ‘nasty’ iCloud storage scam
stories.summary: The scam uses panic around cloud storage limits to trick users into handing over personal and financial information.
stories.source_signal_title: ‘Your photos will be deleted’: Apple users warned over ‘nasty’ iCloud storage scam
body: Apple users are being warned about a scam that threatens to delete photos unless victims respond, exploiting the panic many people feel about cloud storage and personal data. The fraud appears designed to pressure users into handing over information quickly before they have time to check it.

The danger is not only financial but personal, because access to cloud accounts can expose images, contacts and other sensitive material. Scams that use fear of losing photos or files are effective precisely because they target something people instinctively want to protect.

The warning is a reminder that cybercrime increasingly relies on psychological manipulation rather than technical sophistication alone. Users who receive such messages should treat them with caution, because urgent threats are often designed to create exactly the sort of rushed response scammers need.

stories.title: Dancer with MND performs on stage again through digital avatar
stories.summary: Brainwave and avatar technology have enabled a performer with motor neurone disease to return to the stage, offering a striking example of assistive tech in practice.
stories.source_signal_title: Dancer with MND performs on stage again through digital avatar
body: A dancer with motor neurone disease has returned to the stage through a digital avatar created using brainwave technology, in a striking example of assistive technology in performance. The project allowed the artist to reclaim a form of expression that illness had taken away.

The significance goes beyond one performance. It shows how emerging technology can help people with severe physical conditions remain creatively active, not just communicate or move through daily life. In that sense, the work offers a glimpse of a future in which digital tools extend participation as well as convenience.

There are still limits, of course, because an avatar cannot fully replace bodily presence. But the project matters because it demonstrates that technology can restore a degree of agency and connection in places where medicine alone cannot.

stories.title: Man City smell blood - why Arsenal should fear title rivals
stories.summary: Manchester City’s win has tightened the title race and made their challenge look increasingly credible as Arsenal come under pressure.
stories.source_signal_title: Man City smell blood - why Arsenal should fear title rivals
body: Manchester City’s latest win has tightened the Premier League title race and increased the pressure on Arsenal. With the gap narrowing and momentum shifting, Pep Guardiola’s side now look capable of turning a contest into a chase.

The result matters because City have a long record of closing out seasons strongly, while Arsenal are now vulnerable to any slip-up. In a race this tight, form and confidence can matter as much as points, and City’s recent surge has made them feel like the team to beat.

For Arsenal, the concern is not just the scoreboard but the sense that their rivals are finding their rhythm at the decisive moment. If City keep winning, the title race could quickly become one defined by Arsenal’s need to respond rather than dictate.

stories.title: Van Aert beats Pogacar in thrilling Paris-Roubaix sprint
stories.summary: Wout van Aert’s victory in one of cycling’s most demanding races was a major result in a marquee classic.
stories.source_signal_title: Van Aert beats Pogacar in thrilling Paris-Roubaix sprint
body: Wout van Aert has won Paris-Roubaix after beating Tadej Pogacar in a dramatic sprint finish, a major result in one of cycling’s most demanding one-day races. The contest lived up to the classic’s reputation for attrition, dust and tactical brutality.

The victory matters because Roubaix is one of the sport’s most prestigious prizes and because Pogacar’s presence raised the stakes even further. Van Aert’s ability to outlast and outkick one of the modern era’s strongest riders gives the win particular weight.

It also reinforces how unpredictable the cobbled monuments can be, even when the strongest names are in the race. In a season where every major result is closely scrutinised, this one will stand out as both a marquee win and a reminder of van Aert’s range.

stories.title: Will Romero's tears become the enduring image of Tottenham's season?
stories.summary: Tottenham’s defeat deepens the sense of crisis, with relegation now a live concern and pressure building on the new manager.
stories.source_signal_title: Will Romero's tears become the enduring image of Tottenham's season?
body: Cristian Romero’s tears after Tottenham’s defeat have the potential to become the defining image of a desperate season, one now shadowed by the threat of relegation. The emotional reaction captured the sense of collapse around the club more starkly than the scoreline alone.

The result matters because it leaves the new manager Roberto De Zerbi under immediate pressure and deepens anxiety among supporters who had hoped for a stabilising change. Instead, Tottenham remain in the bottom three, with little margin for error.

What happens next will depend on whether the team can recover quickly enough to stop the season becoming a fight for survival. If not, Romero’s visible distress may come to symbolise a campaign in which crisis has overtaken expectation.

stories.title: Sinner beats Alcaraz to return to world top spot
stories.summary: Jannik Sinner has reclaimed world number one after beating Carlos Alcaraz, a significant shift at the top of men’s tennis.
stories.source_signal_title: Sinner beats Alcaraz to return to world top spot
body: Jannik Sinner has beaten Carlos Alcaraz to reclaim the world number one ranking, a significant shift at the top of men’s tennis. The result confirms Sinner’s rise as a leading force in the sport and adds another chapter to a rivalry that looks set to define the years ahead.

The match matters because both players are still young enough to shape the next era, yet already established enough for their meetings to carry major ranking and symbolic weight. In that context, this was more than a title win: it was a statement about momentum and durability.

What comes next is the usual question in tennis after a change at the top: whether the new order lasts. For now, Sinner has the advantage, but the balance between him and Alcaraz remains one of the sport’s most compelling contests.
